words: SAM SCHRAGER: The fact that the homesteading that you and your husband did
was so late. How do you explain that there was still this land av
ailable.
LORA ALBRIGHT: Nobody wanted it; it's rough land, it was real rough land, and
when Raleigh came invalided home from Moscow, it was about 1916- it
was about two years before, let's see, we were married in '18, there
was a man who had filed on it and then threw it up because he didn't
want it. And this is true, because the place where the Johnson is, Mc
that white house, is the old Albright house. They, too bought an
exemption from the person that had filed earlier. Andhis person-fhoped
that there would be coal; there was a big rush, and the old
mine hole, they thought there was coal in the area, because when you
go from here to Lewiston you see.black stuff that came down. Well,
and there is coalbut it such small veins, so then they threw it
up when it didn't do anything. And, Raleigh had filed,bought the
exemption for twenty-five dollars. And then he took it over because
itjoined his parents' pasture. And then, after we lived on it and
proved up on it, a hundred and sixty acres, then we took another op
tion on this government land over here. And that's why it was late,
because it's rough land.

speaker: SS
words: When you say rough—

speaker: LA
words: Hilly, stony, just like this back there, look out there Sam. You

speaker: LA
words: see how it is? And the only thing you ever could do on it, would

speaker: LA
words: be to pasture it, or some of the people thought at one time that

speaker: LA
words: they could plant trees. Now, the pine trees Agrow. Apple trees,

speaker: LA
words: seedling apple trees have grown up there and you know how it is,

speaker: LA
words: people that don't have land, and have a chance to get some free

speaker: LA
words: land, and some of the fellows thought they could prove up on it, and

speaker: LA
words: some of them did. And then got a job in Lewiston and would work,

speaker: LA
words: or in Moscow and work. And then another thing, we had the rural

speaker: LA
words: schools and it was easier for them to send their kids to rural schools

speaker: LA
words: than it was to move to Lewiston and attend the school, and this sort

speaker: LA
words: of thing. And so, all they had to do was live there, if they could

speaker: LA
words: make a living anywhere, and they could have cows and chickensand a

speaker: LA
words: garden, if they could find a spot little enough and level enough.

speaker: LA
words: Our homestead shack was across in a gulch, and it was fourteen by

speaker: LA
words: sixteen; Raleigh went up there with a pick and a shovel and an old

speaker: LA
words: horse and dug out a place and we put the corner of that twelve by

speaker: LA
words: sixteen— this corner was about six feet in the ground and that cor

speaker: LA
words: ner was eight feet off the ground on a post. And we lived there and

speaker: LA
words: proved up on it. Lived there two and a half years. You had to move

speaker: LA
words: onto it in the first six months, you see, and it took three years

speaker: LA
words: to prove up on 'em. And we carried in our water, because the only

speaker: LA
words: water that was available was in the creek and that wasn't fit to drink.

speaker: LA
words: So, we carried it in bottles from over at the homestead and carried

speaker: LA
words: in our food. And, it wasn't too bad. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: How far did you have to hike in then with the water? Was this from

speaker: SS
words: Raleigh's parents' place?

speaker: LA
words: Yeah. Oh, what? Quarter of a mile.

speaker: SS
words: Uphill, pretty much?

speaker: LA
words: Oh, no, along the hillside. Oh, a little bit, but then, you start

speaker: LA
words: from the river and then just gradually angle off round there, it

speaker: LA
words: wasn't too difficult.

speaker: SS
words: Twelve by sixteen—

speaker: LA
words: Fourteen by sixteen.

speaker: SS
words: Im talking about the size of the lookout that I had.

speaker: LA
words: Oh, yes. Well, we had a little tiny stove, a little homesteader's

speaker: LA
words: stove with four holes and a little tiny oven. And, I'm a tall girl

speaker: LA
words: so Raleigh put it up on a stand. He built a wooden stand, and we

speaker: LA
words: put the four little legs up there so that I could cook without bending

speaker: LA
words: over. And that :of the cupboard was my kitchen and I

speaker: LA
words: had the spices and the flour and the stuff in a little thing about

speaker: LA
words: this big, and the dishes were next to it, and the table was right

speaker: LA
words: next to  And that was right by the foot of the bed. Then we had

speaker: LA
words: a big shelf, that Raleigh put up, with a curtain around it, and that

speaker: LA
words: was our clothescloset. And on top of it was our, oh, we put hats

speaker: LA
words: and we put special books, and then on the other corner that we had—

speaker: LA
words: this big chair, by the way— Raleigh went — was in Moscow and he

speaker: LA
words: went to a sale, a furniture sale that was selling out up there, and

speaker: LA
words: for fifteen dollars he bought this little chair and it's solid oak.

speaker: LA
words: This is the original seat, we've had it or three times

speaker: LA
words: and I see it's got aVAin here, but then, after fifty-eight years,

speaker: LA
words: what do you expect? And this is the only thingI brought. This is

speaker: LA
words: the thing we took with us. And I've rocked all my children in that.

speaker: LA
words: We had a cradle, of course, and over the bed when the children came

speaker: LA
words: we strung a clothesbasket, you know Papa and Mama could sleep down

speaker: LA
words: here and the baby was in the clothesbasket. (Chuckles) You have to

speaker: LA
words: be very inventive, Sam, when you live . And Raleigh had put a

speaker: LA
words: nice floor in it. Sometimes it was real rough. Some of the shacks

speaker: LA
words: thalk've seen, they were board with cracks in them, but Raleigh was

speaker: LA
words: a perfectionist and his father was a carpenter, and so because the

speaker: LA
words: bride was a schoolteacher, they put a good floor in it, which I was

speaker: LA
words: very thankful for. And then we had construction— you may not know

speaker: LA
words: what construction paper is- it was a felt paper- on the inside a

speaker: LA
words: felt paper that used to be laid under shingles on the outside of a

speaker: LA
words: house and sometimes on the inside they would put little panelling

speaker: LA
words: up and put this paper under there. Well, that was the only paper

speaker: LA
words: we had, and we never did get the inside and just tarpaper on

speaker: LA
words: the outside with laths on it, and one inch boards. But, it was warm

speaker: LA
words: because of the paper.We got along very well. After the other chil

speaker: LA
words: dren came, why, we- every time we had anew baby we'd put a shed on

speaker: LA
words: one side or the other. But, we moved down- that wasn't the original

speaker: LA
words: shack because we rented Indian land that was next to it, where we

speaker: LA
words: could get a little bit closer because we had to get our children out

speaker: LA
words: to school. And so, that was the one that we put the additions on.

speaker: SS
words: So the original cabin you never did enlarge.

speaker: LA
words: No, we never enlarged that.

speaker: SS
words: You did have your first children there?

speaker: LA
words: No. The baby was not born there because my mother-in-law was bound

speaker: LA
words: and determined that I should have a doctor in attendance, naturally.

speaker: LA
words: So, the doctor wouldn't go over there, so we went over to her house

speaker: LA
words: and the baby was born there. And the three children were born there

speaker: LA
words: and then the last baby was born in the homestead that we had on the

speaker: LA
words: Indian place and that's just right up here —

speaker: SS
words: How many years did you actually live in your own homestead cabin?

speaker: SS
words: On your homestead.

speaker: LA
words: We actually lived there about two years, really, to stay there. We because

speaker: LA
words: Raleigh- we had to make a living, and there was a crop fail

speaker: LA
words: ure that third year, so he went back to the mechanical work and went

speaker: LA
words: to Troy andspent the summer there in Troy, because there was about

speaker: LA
words: a hundred and sixty dollars that we had to pay, you know for theJ

speaker: LA
words: I can't remember just what that was— it was in lieu of fences, be

speaker: LA
words: cause the fences were on top of that hill up there, and there's no

speaker: LA
words: road up there, and we didn't have any horse at the time, we had to

speaker: LA
words: use my father-in-law's horses; and how do you get a horse up there

speaker: LA
words: to take posts and wire and so forth? There was only three acres up

speaker: LA
words: there, so we did till it, but we didn't fence it, and in lieu of

speaker: LA
words: that, why then, we could pay an extra— there was an extra amount

speaker: LA
words: there. And Raleigh went to Troy and he was one of the original good

speaker: LA
words: auto mechanics, and so he earned enough money to pay our— to pay

speaker: LA
words: that and buy our food for the winter. And soothe next year, we rent

speaker: LA
words: ed the Indian land, because Raleigh had been in the market garden

speaker: LA
words: ing business and this was a good thing for him, and he was outdoors

speaker: LA
words: where he needed to be. And so, that's when we movedthe shack down

speaker: LA
words: on the river bank, over here on the Indian land. We still used the

speaker: LA
words: homestead for pasture, which was as intended in the first place, and

speaker: LA
words: we were lucky enough to pick up some old range cows that they really

speaker: LA
words: were fattening for butcher. And Raleigh found out that they were

speaker: LA
words: bred and so he came home and he said, "You know, if we could get

speaker: LA
words: those cows— if we could afford to get those cows— we would have

speaker: LA
words: the calves and then if one of them turned out to be a milker, we'd

speaker: LA
words: have a milkcow for the youngsters. So, I took the last hundred and

speaker: LA
words: twelve dollars of the money I'd saved from teaching school and we

speaker: LA
words: bought those cows. By golly, they all had calves! And this is the

speaker: LA
words: way you get along. So, that's that.

speaker: SS
words: Your husband's inclination to farm: was farming what he really

speaker: SS
words: wanted to do, more than mechanicing?

speaker: LA
words: No, he would rather have been a mechanic, but he had a disability

speaker: LA
words: that he couldn't. In the old days, you know, mechanics instead of

speaker: LA
words: having hoists that they could put the cars up, they had to lay on

speaker: LA
words: a roller thing, you know, on the concrete floor. When he was a kid,

speaker: LA
words: that was before I knew him, the railroad people had their own fences

speaker: LA
words: and there was a pipe gate, like this, with barb wire through it, and

speaker: LA
words: kidlike, he was going after the cows and he decided he wouldn't bo

speaker: LA
words: ther to open the gate and jumped over it, and he fell and dislocated and hit his side

speaker: LA
words: and dislocated those vertebrae; bothered him

speaker: LA
words: all his life and he could no longer lay on the concrete and do the

speaker: LA
words: work that he was supposed to do. And the doctor told him— he had

speaker: LA
words: this good friend in Moscow that was a doctor and he used to drive

speaker: LA
words: for him because he needed to be out in the country, and Raleigh was

speaker: LA
words: a good driver.

speaker: SS
words: Doctor?

speaker: LA
words: Oh, the hospital, what's that name?

speaker: SS
words: Gritman?

speaker: LA
words: Yeah, Gritman. Yeah, Raleigh drove Dr. Gritman in his early days.

speaker: SS
words: And Dr. Gritman had probably the first car in the area. I am told

speaker: SS
words: about him driving all around the country.

speaker: LA
words: Okay. Well, Raleigh was the person that drove him and taught him

speaker: LA
words: to drive and when he 4 drive he drove him. And he kept the

speaker: LA
words: car in excellent condition and he liked it. And it was Dr. Gritman

speaker: LA
words: that finally said, "Kid,you can't do this. You've got to get out.

speaker: LA
words: You've got to get away from here. You can't do this, because your

speaker: LA
words: kidneys are in such shape that I won't guarantee you i more

speaker: LA
words: than five years." Well, it scared Raleigh, naturally, and so his

speaker: LA
words: folks, then said, "Why don't you come on home? You'll be out and

speaker: LA
words: you can help us on the market gardening." And so, when I came down

speaker: LA
words: he was working with his own People at market gardening. And then

speaker: LA
words: when it turned out that I would stay and that we would be married,

speaker: LA
words: we had to make some provisions. Well, Raleigh already had this thing

speaker: LA
words: so Pa and all of 'em got together and they built that little cabin

speaker: LA
words: over there, because we were supposed to live on it. Raleigh, before

speaker: LA
words: he was married wouldn't have to because his home was with his people.

speaker: LA
words: But after you get married, it changes your status. And the we thought

speaker: LA
words: of putting up a tent, but I wasn't too happy with a tent. My motherin-

speaker: LA
words: law says, "Oh, no, my goodness, you're not going to live in a tent-"

speaker: LA
words: So, she was the one that put the finger on the boys to get the lum

speaker: LA
words: ber over there. And tht is what happened.

speaker: SS
words: So, the cabin was built by the family, really?

speaker: LA
words: Yes. Oh, yes. I didn't do anything with it at all. I went home that

speaker: LA
words: summer and then that spring, I had a seven month's school there, and

speaker: LA
words: I was home a month , and then the 7th of May we were married and I

speaker: LA
words: came back and the cabin was all built. But, no one lived in it but

speaker: LA
words: us. After we left, we left some stuff stored there, and the first

speaker: LA
words: cycldne that I ever saw in this country, blew it down. Scattered it

speaker: LA
words: all up and down. And my sterling silverware was scattered all up and

speaker: LA
words: down the hillside and we had to retrieve it. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: I didn't know there were cyclones.

speaker: LA
words: We have terrible winds here. When I said cyclones, you were think

speaker: LA
words: ing of a tornado thing.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: LA
words: No, a cyclonic wind is just a terrible wind. In fact, last Easter

speaker: LA
words: Sunday we had another one. We seem to get it in the spring. Two

speaker: LA
words: Easter Sundays, and it wrecked my grandson's building over here. He

speaker: LA
words: felt real badly about it because he says, "The only things are wrecked

speaker: LA
words: are the things I built." Well, ho built an open shed, and he thought

speaker: LA
words: he had the studs buried deep enough, but he didn't, and the wind

speaker: LA
words: just naturally pulled 'em off and blew it over behind the old build

speaker: LA
words: ing and smashed it away.

speaker: SS
words: The same thing happened the other night on the ridge where I live

speaker: SS
words: and it blew off part of the roof on the barn, and it went smashing

speaker: SS
words: into an old truck that we have and bent the frame on the cab.

speaker: LA
words: And which ridge is that?

speaker: SS
words: Little Bear.

speaker: LA
words: Oh, Little Bear Ridge. Well, then you know.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, it's bad.

speaker: LA
words: Was this just this past ?

speaker: SS
words: This was the night before last.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, it was! Oh, that's part of that wind— the blizzard that we had

speaker: SS
words: in Montana, then. But they said that the storm was going down— you

speaker: SS
words: see, where Helena is, up there, goes down the Pocatello way, instead

speaker: SS
words: of coming down our way, and it was real wind.

speaker: SS
words: Let me ask you: How you decided you were interested in becoming a

speaker: SS
words: teacher.

speaker: SS
words: I was the oldest of a large family, and as the oldest girl with an

speaker: SS
words: ill mother, I had been taking charge of the family more or less, and

speaker: SS
words: I graduated from high school at Gifford, c? tne first class in the

speaker: SS
words: Gifford High School, way back in 1916. And, I had been teaching,

speaker: SS
words: filling in, a long time, and I found that it was just real fun to

speaker: SS
words: teach the lower grades while I was this high school kid, you know.

speaker: SS
words: There's just something— the gleam in a youngsters eye when he catches

speaker: SS
words: that gleam of what you're trying to teach him. And, I needed to

speaker: SS
words: make some moneyj to get a job and make some money. Because my mother

speaker: SS
words: was a teacher she encouraged me, so I went to Moscow and took the

speaker: SS
words: examination and taught. That's how I got my and I loved it, I

speaker: SS
words: loved to teach. I taught four terms before I was married. And

speaker: SS
words: when I say four terms— I taught two full terms and then filled in

speaker: SS
words: for other teachers that for some reason or other didn't fill their-

speaker: SS
words: In fact, one year I taught my own seven month's term in

speaker: SS
words: Jack's Canyon up here, a little rural school. I went home to Look

speaker: SS
words: out and that teacher had— was ill and couldn't finish, and accor

speaker: SS
words: ding to the law they had to have seven months, and there was still

speaker: SS
words: three monthsthat wasn't finished. And then, that was the reason

speaker: SS
words: that I came down here, because that teacher ran away and got mar

speaker: SS
words: ried and she didn't finish hers, so I had to teach six weeks there

speaker: SS
words: to finish out seven months here. And, so, that's the way. Then,

speaker: SS
words: that fall— I wasn't going to teach at all, because my mother was

speaker: SS
words: still bedridden and was ill, and because teachers were scarce—

speaker: SS
words: you see, we were teaching for sixty and sixty-five dollars a month,

speaker: SS
words: and most of the young people were getting more than that at the timeand

speaker: SS
words: I had planned to stay home and take care of my mother. Then we

speaker: SS
words: had a new county superintendent, a Mrs. Foster, and I think if

speaker: SS
words: Miss Redfield had stayed, she knew the situation, and would not have

speaker: SS
words: the pressure on me, but Mrs. Foster sent me a kind of a nasty letter

speaker: SS
words: saying, "Well, the State has granted you a certificate, and if you're

speaker: SS
words: not going to use it, maybe we should give it to someone else." It

speaker: SS
words: wasn't quite that bald, but it frightened me because I had spent my

speaker: SS
words: savings in summer school and had raised my certificate from a third

speaker: SS
words: grade to a second grade. So, I showed it to my father and he said,

speaker: SS
words: "Well, Honey, I guess you'd better go." So, I took the next train

speaker: SS
words: in the middle of October and came down here and I've been here ever

speaker: SS
words: since. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: This job was waiting for you then?

speaker: LA
words: I guess so. I guess so. I had all grades. It's a good thing I'd

speaker: LA
words: had before. I had eighteen pupils; all grades and ages from five

speaker: LA
words: and a half to eighteen. You see, that's the rural school that you

speaker: LA
words: get into and it takes some doing to keep that many pupils interested

speaker: LA
words: and occupied and so on. But pupils in a rural school, as a rule,

speaker: LA
words: will oh, look after little kids. These youngsters that were so young

speaker: LA
words: were walking two and a half miles in snow and sleet. And, no buses

speaker: LA
words: in those days, you know. And so, two of 44 children would see

speaker: LA
words: that they got home, because I was worried, too, you can't turn a five

speaker: LA
words: osix year kid out in a storm to walk three or four miles.

speaker: SS
words: What was the name of the school?

speaker: LA
words: Pilot Rock. Now, when you came down from Moscow—

speaker: SS
words: I came through Juliaetta.

speaker: LA
words: When you come from Juliaetta, you didn't realize when you were going

speaker: LA
words: across the bridge to that little creek and then that big rocky thing

speaker: LA
words: comes down, you go around and you saw avhome there, well, that's my

speaker: LA
words: old schoolhouse. That's the old Pilot Rock schoolhouse. When we

speaker: LA
words: consolidated in 1949-1951, I worked on the county committee- I'd

speaker: LA
words: been forty-one years in School Board work, after I quit teaching they

speaker: LA
words: put me on the School Board. And so I worked there and so I was

speaker: LA
words: named a member of the Consolidation Committee for Nez Perce County,

speaker: LA
words: and I was there to consolidate that rural school, you see, wilh the

speaker: LA
words: Lapwai District. And, it was interesting to me, because in the old

speaker: LA
words: days, I had tried to interest them in consolidating with Juliaetta,

speaker: LA
words: which seemed fine; they already had buses coming down off the ridges

speaker: LA
words: and I thought, "Well, we're close enough, why not take those pupils

speaker: LA
words: up there?" And then I worked on the Board to consolidate and sent

speaker: LA
words: 'em clear over to Because Juliaetta was consolidated wiht Kendrick,

speaker: LA
words: see.

speaker: SS
words: Well, your background what you had to learn to teach— you took summer

speaker: SS
words: school courses? Is that.way you got there? You did this at the

speaker: SS
words: University at Moscow?

speaker: LA
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Did this take very long, to be?

speaker: LA
words: Six weeks— six to nine weeks. Its a summer course. I would go in

speaker: LA
words: about in June and stay til, oh, the last of July or the middle of Aug

speaker: LA
words: ust, and then— Well, you had to work, but, it wasn't any harder,

speaker: LA
words: really than for me to prepare and have the lessons ready for eight

speaker: LA
words: grades of school teaching! And, we were supposed to have a lesson

speaker: LA
words: plan, so that when our District Superintendent would come to visit,

speaker: LA
words: it was posted on the wall. I usually wrote right on the :f?oard,

speaker: LA
words: because I change it. And you had to coordinate and corelate

speaker: LA
words: the seven subjects from the fifth grade to the eighth grade. They

speaker: LA
words: all had their own degree of geography, reading, spelling, physiology.

speaker: LA
words: You went through that, didn't you? Or didn't you have all those things

speaker: LA
words: when you went to school?

speaker: SS
words: I had that pretty much.

speaker: LA
words: And so, I would corelate. Now for instance, in physiology, I would—

speaker: LA
words: a physiology book that is right for a seventh and eighth gradej they

speaker: LA
words: have to read that, and so I would have that as a reading lesson. And

speaker: LA
words: then I could— and then spelling, sometimes I would put the fourth and

speaker: LA
words: fifth grades together, and they would have spelling, and then I would

speaker: LA
words: appoint some of the youngsters, that would like to, to hear this spel

speaker: LA
words: ling. And it was interesting because they had to correct it. And if

speaker: LA
words: they didn't correct it, why then, they got a mark down. I mean, if

speaker: LA
words: they didn't notice that it was spelled wrong, and we had spelling books

speaker: LA
words: in those days. It wasn't all oral spelling. And so, it was easy to

speaker: LA
words: delegate some of And then we had to teach penmanship. Well,

speaker: LA
words: I had to work that in, too, because, some of those— you'd be surprised

speaker: LA
words: the sixth graders would be better penmen at old Spencerian, where

speaker: LA
words: you did it, you know— so the youngsters— And, I'd make a test of it.

speaker: LA
words: Not real school, because you can't expect a fourth and fifth grader whtcn

speaker: LA
words: are getting to that age where they're pretty independent, to look to a

speaker: LA
words: seventh and eighth grader with authority unless they want to. So, we

speaker: LA
words: would have spelling bees, and we would have writing bees and we would

speaker: LA
words: have afternoon reading hours and invite the mothers in and,

speaker: LA
words: oh, I did all kinds of things.

speaker: SS
words: You had the parents come in and take part in it?

speaker: LA
words: Well, listen, some of them did that were interested, and some of them

speaker: LA
words: couldn't be bothered, you know how it is. But the teacher was always

speaker: LA
words: expected to have a Literary, too. Every week. Oh, boy! And so, I'd

speaker: LA
words: say, "Well, look, Elmer, for instance, he was a slow learner. If

speaker: LA
words: you would learn this verse of six lines, you can be in the Literary

speaker: LA
words: Friday." Well, sometimes it worked. And then, the singing, we had

speaker: LA
words: an old organ there. Thank goodness I'd had a little family music les

speaker: LA
words: sons, and so, we diAour singing. And two or three of the youngsters

speaker: LA
words: would be a little better, and so, we'd have some solos and we had

speaker: LA
words: some duets, and — The schoolteachers in those days were looked to

speaker: LA
words: for— I wonder how to say this— for the intellectual leadership of a

speaker: LA
words: community. And this Literary business was part of it. And, the part

speaker: LA
words: that I didn't like was, every Friday night they had a debate. And this

speaker: LA
words: was for the— not for the school children— but for the community.

speaker: LA
words: And, my trouble with the debate, was to keep it from being too personal.

speaker: LA
words: Acrimonious. And to keep the themes decent. You get into a back

speaker: LA
